segments.text: There are over 5,000 basketball-sized spheres deep under the ice in Antarctica trying to detect something that no one
can see. This is one of the largest science experiments in the world and it was nearly impossible to build because
drilling into Antarctica is hard. The first 45 m down is firmly packed snow.
So, they use a drill like this. Copper pipes full of boiling water make it super hot, melting its way through the snow. But then you hit solid ice and you
need to switch to a different kind of drill that instead of just being hot shoots out boiling water at 760 L per
minute to melt right through. Then they need sensors like this to measure the hole's diameter to make sure it stays a consistent size. And don't even get me
started on how they get hot water to the coldest, driest, windiest place on Earth in the first place. It took over 2 days
and tons of resources to drill a single hole. But they did it 86 times, spanning enough ice to fill a million swimming
pools. And then they connected thousands of detectors on cables and dropped them into the holes where they now sit frozen
and isolated in total darkness waiting for something deep out in space. to find out what they're waiting for.
